title: 3 - Science (Grade 1)
outcomeText: 6-5-1-1-2 Identifying the major structural and func-tional differences between animal and plant cells.

questions.question: What is the main function of photosynthesis in plant cells?
options: B. To produce energy from sunlight
questions.explanation: Photosynthesis is the process by which plant cells produce energy from sunlight, water, and carbon dioxide.

questions.question: What is the main function of diffusion in animal cells?
options: C. To move molecules from high to low concentration
questions.explanation: Diffusion is the process by which molecules move from an area of high concentration to an area of low concentration in animal cells.

questions.question: What is the main function of passive transport in animal cells?
options: C. To move molecules without energy
questions.explanation: Passive transport is the process by which molecules move into or out of animal cells without the use of energy.

questions.question: What is the main function of cellular respiration in plant cells?
options: A. To produce energy from sunlight
questions.explanation: Cellular respiration is the process by which plant cells break down glucose to produce energy.

questions.question: What is the main difference between animal and plant cells?
options: A. Animal cells have a nucleus, plant cells do not
B. Plant cells have chloroplasts, animal cells do not
C. Animal cells have mitochondria, plant cells do not
D. Plant cells have a cell wall, animal cells do not
questions.explanation: Plant cells have chloroplasts, which are responsible for photosynthesis, while animal cells do not.

questions.question: What is the main function of chloroplasts in plant cells?
options: B. To produce energy from sunlight
questions.explanation: Chloroplasts are organelles found in plant cells that are responsible for photosynthesis.

questions.question: What is the main function of the cell wall in plant cells?
options: B. To produce energy from sunlight
questions.explanation: The cell wall is a rigid layer found outside the cell membrane of plant cells that provides structural support.

questions.question: What is the main function of the nucleus in animal cells?
options: A. To produce energy from sunlight
questions.explanation: The nucleus is the organelle found in animal cells that stores genetic information.

questions.question: What is the main function of mitochondria in animal cells?
options: A. To produce energy from sunlight
questions.explanation: Mitochondria are organelles found in animal cells that are responsible for cellular respiration.

questions.question: What is the main function of the cell membrane in animal cells?
options: A. To produce energy from sunlight
B. To control the movement of molecules
questions.explanation: The cell membrane is a thin layer that surrounds the cell and controls the movement of molecules in and out of the cell.

title: 1 - Science (Grade 1)
outcomeText: 6-5-1-1-3 Identifying the main body systems and their specialized organs and linking them to their func-tions that supports growth and survival of living organ-isms (plants and animals).

questions.question: What is the main function of the digestive system in an animal's body?
options: C. To break down food into nutrients
questions.explanation: The digestive system breaks down food into nutrients that can be used by the body for growth and energy.

questions.question: What is the main function of the circulatory system in an animal's body?
options: A. To break down food into nutrients
C. To transport oxygen and nutrients to cells
D. To remove waste from the body
questions.explanation: The circulatory system transports oxygen and nutrients to cells and removes waste from the body.

questions.question: What is the main function of the excretory system in an animal's body?
options: A. To transport oxygen and nutrients to cells
B. To break down food into nutrients
C. To remove waste from the body
questions.explanation: The excretory system removes waste from the body through processes such as urination and defecation.

questions.question: What is the main function of the respiratory system in an animal's body?
options: A. To break down food into nutrients
B. To remove waste from the body
C. To transport oxygen to cells
questions.explanation: The respiratory system brings oxygen into the body and removes carbon dioxide through processes such as breathing.

questions.question: What is the main function of the skeletal system in an animal's body?
options: A. To transport oxygen and nutrients to cells
B. To break down food into nutrients
C. To provide support and protection for the body
D. To remove waste from the body
questions.explanation: The skeletal system provides support and protection for the body and allows for movement through the action of muscles.

questions.question: What is the main function of the muscular system in an animal's body?
options: A. To transport oxygen and nutrients to cells
B. To break down food into nutrients
C. To move the body and maintain posture
D. To remove waste from the body
questions.explanation: The muscular system allows for movement and maintains posture by contracting and relaxing muscles.

questions.question: What is the main function of the nervous system in an animal's body?
options: A. To transport oxygen and nutrients to cells
B. To break down food into nutrients
C. To control movements and transmit signals
D. To remove waste from the body
questions.explanation: The nervous system controls movements and transmits signals through processes such as nerve impulses.

questions.question: What is the main function of the skin in an animal's body?
options: A. To break down food into nutrients
B. To remove waste from the body
C. To regulate body temperature and protect the body
D. To transport oxygen and nutrients to cells
questions.explanation: The skin regulates body temperature and protects the body from external damage.

questions.question: What is the main function of the eyes in an animal's body?
options: A. To break down food into nutrients
B. To remove waste from the body
C. To detect light and transmit visual information
D. To transport oxygen and nutrients to cells
questions.explanation: The eyes detect light and transmit visual information to the brain for interpretation.

questions.question: What is the main function of the ears in an animal's body?
options: A. To break down food into nutrients
B. To remove waste from the body
C. To detect sound and transmit auditory information
D. To transport oxygen and nutrients to cells
questions.explanation: The ears detect sound and transmit auditory information to the brain for interpretation.

title: 2 - Science (Grade 1)
outcomeText: 6-5-1-1-3 Identifying the main body systems and their specialized organs and linking them to their func-tions that supports growth and survival of living organ-isms (plants and animals).

questions.question: What is the main function of the root of a plant?
options: A. To make food for the plant
B. To hold the plant upright
C. To absorb water and nutrients from the soil
questions.explanation: The root of a plant absorbs water and nutrients from the soil, which helps the plant grow and survive.

questions.question: What is the main function of the stem of a plant?
options: A. To make food for the plant
B. To hold the plant upright
C. To store water and nutrients
questions.explanation: The stem of a plant holds the plant upright and supports its growth.

questions.question: What is the main function of the leaf of a plant?
options: B. To make food for the plant
C. To hold the plant upright
D. To absorb water and nutrients from the soil
questions.explanation: The leaf of a plant makes food for the plant through a process called photosynthesis.

questions.question: What is the main function of the flower of a plant?

B. To hold the plant upright
C. To absorb water and nutrients from the soil
questions.explanation: The flower of a plant produces seeds, which helps the plant reproduce and grow.

questions.question: What part of a plant absorbs water and nutrients from the soil?

questions.question: What part of a plant holds the plant upright?

questions.question: What part of a plant makes food for the plant?
questions.explanation: The leaf of a plant makes food for the plant through a process called photosynthesis.

questions.question: What is the main function of the root, stem, and leaf of a plant?
options: B. To hold the plant upright

questions.question: What is the main function of the flower of a plant?
options: A. To hold the plant upright
B. To absorb water and nutrients from the soil
C. To make food for the plant
questions.explanation: The flower of a plant produces seeds, which helps the plant reproduce and grow.

questions.question: What part of a plant produces seeds?

title: 1 - Science (Grade 1)
outcomeText: 6-5-1-2-1 Classifying living organisms into groups based on common phenotypic traits.

questions.question: What are the two main groups of plants in our local environment?
questions.explanation: Gymnosperms and Angiosperms are the two main groups of plants in our local environment.

questions.question: Which group of plants has seeds covered by a hard outer layer?
questions.explanation: Angiosperms have seeds covered by a hard outer layer.

questions.question: Which group of plants produces cones?
questions.explanation: Gymnosperms produce cones.

questions.question: Which group of plants has flowers?
questions.explanation: Angiosperms have flowers.

questions.question: Which group of plants has seeds without a hard outer layer?
questions.explanation: Gymnosperms have seeds without a hard outer layer.

questions.question: What is the main difference between Gymnosperms and Angiosperms?
options: Gymnosperms have flowers, Angiosperms have cones
Gymnosperms have cones, Angiosperms have flowers
Gymnosperms produce seeds without a hard outer layer, Angiosperms produce seeds with a hard outer layer
Gymnosperms have seeds with a hard outer layer, Angiosperms have seeds without a hard outer layer
questions.explanation: Gymnosperms produce seeds without a hard outer layer, Angiosperms produce seeds with a hard outer layer.

questions.question: Which group of plants has seeds that can be dispersed by wind?
questions.explanation: Gymnosperms have seeds that can be dispersed by wind.

questions.question: Which group of plants has seeds that can be dispersed by animals?
questions.explanation: Angiosperms have seeds that can be dispersed by animals.

questions.question: Which group of plants produces seeds that can grow into new plants?
questions.explanation: Angiosperms produce seeds that can grow into new plants.

questions.question: Which group of plants has seeds that are not produced in cones?
questions.explanation: Angiosperms have seeds that are not produced in cones.

title: 3 - Science (Grade 1)
outcomeText: 6-5-1-3-1 Representing biological communities and identifying types of population that lives in it, describ-ing the interrelationships among them, and their inter-action with the abiotic components, and the impact of biological communities changes on their survival and sustainability.

questions.question: What happens to the rabbit population if its favorite food, clover, disappears?
options: A. The rabbit population will increase.
B. The rabbit population will decrease.
C. The rabbit population will stay the same.
D. The rabbit population will move to a different area.
questions.explanation: Rabbits rely on clover as a primary food source. Without it, their population will decrease.

questions.question: What will happen to the fish if the water in their pond becomes polluted?
options: A. The fish population will increase.
B. The fish population will decrease.
C. The fish population will stay the same.
D. The fish will not be affected.
questions.explanation: Pollution in the water can harm or kill fish, leading to a decrease in their population.

questions.question: If a tree is cut down in a forest, what will happen to the birds that live in it?
options: A. The birds will find a new home.
B. The birds will stay in the same tree.
C. The birds will increase in number.
questions.explanation: Birds will likely find a new home if their current one is destroyed.

questions.question: What will happen to the bees if their favorite flower, sunflower, is no longer available?
options: A. The bees will find a new flower.
B. The bees will stay with the sunflower.
C. The bees will increase in number.
questions.explanation: Bees will likely find a new flower to visit if their favorite one is no longer available.

questions.question: If a beaver builds a dam in a river, what will happen to the fish that live in it?
options: A. The fish population will increase.
B. The fish population will decrease.
C. The fish population will stay the same.
D. The fish will be unaffected.
questions.explanation: A beaver dam can change the water flow and quality, making it less suitable for fish.

questions.question: What will happen to the squirrels if their favorite tree, oak, is no longer available?
options: A. The squirrels will find a new tree.
B. The squirrels will stay with the oak.
C. The squirrels will increase in number.
questions.explanation: Squirrels will likely find a new tree to live in if their current one is no longer available.

questions.question: If a farmer uses pesticides in his field, what will happen to the butterflies that visit it?
options: A. The butterflies will increase in number.
B. The butterflies will decrease in number.
C. The butterflies will stay the same.
D. The butterflies will be unaffected.
questions.explanation: Pesticides can harm or kill butterflies, leading to a decrease in their population.

questions.question: What will happen to the deer if their favorite food, grass, is no longer available?
options: A. The deer will find a new food source.
B. The deer will stay with the grass.
C. The deer will increase in number.
questions.explanation: Deer will likely find a new food source if their favorite one is no longer available.

questions.question: If a storm hits a forest, what will happen to the trees that are uprooted?
options: A. The trees will grow back stronger.
B. The trees will stay the same.
C. The trees will decrease in number.
D. The trees will be replaced by new ones.
questions.explanation: Trees that are uprooted may not survive and can decrease the forest's tree population.

questions.question: What will happen to the frogs if their pond dries up?
options: A. The frogs will find a new pond.
B. The frogs will stay in the same pond.
C. The frogs will increase in number.
questions.explanation: Frogs rely on a pond for breeding and living. Without it, they will likely disappear.

title: 3 - Science (Grade 1)
outcomeText: 6-5-1-3-2 Describing the components of the ecosystem and explaining the impact of the availability of different resources in ecosystems on the survival and sustainability of living organisms, and proposing solutions to problems that affect the stability of the ecosystem.

questions.question: What happens if a forest has no water?
options: A. The trees will grow faster.
B. The animals will not have enough water to drink.
C. The plants will not grow.
questions.explanation: The animals in the forest will not have enough water to drink, which can lead to dehydration and death.

questions.question: Why is it important to have trees in a forest?
options: A. They provide shelter for animals.
C. They produce oxygen for us to breathe.
D. They protect us from the sun.
questions.explanation: Trees produce oxygen through photosynthesis, which is essential for human and animal survival.

questions.question: What can happen if we pollute a river?
options: A. The fish will grow bigger.
B. The plants will grow faster.
C. The animals will get sick.
questions.explanation: Pollutants in the river can harm animals that live in or drink from it, leading to illness and even death.

questions.question: Why is it important to plant more trees?
options: A. To make the forest smaller.
B. To provide more space for animals.
D. To make the forest disappear.
questions.explanation: Trees absorb carbon dioxide and release oxygen, which can help reduce air pollution and improve air quality.

questions.question: What can happen if a habitat is destroyed?
options: A. The animals will have more space to live.
B. The plants will grow faster.
C. The animals will lose their homes.
D. The animals will not be affected.
questions.explanation: When a habitat is destroyed, animals lose their homes and may struggle to find food and shelter, which can lead to extinction.

questions.question: Why is it important to conserve water?
options: A. So we can swim more.
B. So we can drink more.
C. So we can help plants and animals survive.
D. So we can waste more water.
questions.explanation: Water is essential for plants and animals to survive, and conserving water helps ensure that all living things have enough to drink.

questions.question: What can happen if we cut down too many trees?
options: A. The forest will grow back faster.
B. The animals will have more space to live.
D. The plants will not grow.
questions.explanation: Cutting down too many trees can lead to deforestation, which can cause the forest to disappear and harm the animals that live there.

questions.question: Why is it important to recycle?
options: A. So we can waste more trash.
B. So we can make more pollution.
C. So we can help conserve resources.
D. So we can help the environment.
questions.explanation: Recycling helps conserve resources, reduces waste, and decreases pollution, which can help protect the environment.

questions.question: What can happen if a species becomes extinct?
options: A. It will come back to life.
B. The ecosystem will be balanced.
C. The food chain will be disrupted.
D. The animals will be happy.
questions.explanation: When a species becomes extinct, it can disrupt the food chain and have a ripple effect on the entire ecosystem, leading to instability.

questions.question: Why is it important to protect our oceans?
options: A. So we can pollute them more.
B. So we can waste more plastic.
C. So we can help marine life survive.
D. So we can make more noise pollution.
questions.explanation: Protecting our oceans helps ensure the survival of marine life, which is essential for maintaining the health of our planet.

title: 1 - Science (Grade 1)
outcomeText: 6-5-1-3-3 Representing the relationships between living organisms in which matter is circulated in the ecosystem, and identifying the relationship between plants and energy obtained from the sun to produce food.

questions.question: What do plants make using sunlight?
questions.explanation: Plants make food using sunlight through a process called photosynthesis.

questions.question: What is the main source of energy for plants?
questions.explanation: Plants obtain energy from sunlight through photosynthesis.

questions.question: What is the role of producers in an ecosystem?
questions.explanation: Producers, such as plants, make their own food and form the base of the food chain.

questions.question: What do consumers eat to obtain energy?
questions.explanation: Consumers, such as animals, eat other organisms to obtain energy.

questions.question: What is the role of decomposers in an ecosystem?
options: C. To break down dead organisms
questions.explanation: Decomposers, such as bacteria and fungi, break down dead organisms into nutrients.

questions.question: What is the food chain?
options: A. A group of living organisms that live in the same place
B. A series of organisms that eat each other
C. A type of plant that makes its own food
D. A type of animal that eats plants
questions.explanation: A food chain is a series of organisms that eat each other, with energy passing from one to the next.

questions.question: What is the role of predators in an ecosystem?
options: C. To break down dead organisms
questions.explanation: Predators, such as animals, eat other organisms to obtain energy.

questions.question: How do plants make their own food?
options: B. By breaking down dead organisms
C. By using sunlight, water, and air
D. By using water and soil only
questions.explanation: Plants make their own food through photosynthesis, using sunlight, water, and air.

questions.question: What is the cycle of matter in an ecosystem?
options: A. The movement of energy from one organism to another
B. The movement of water from one organism to another
C. The movement of matter from one organism to another
D. The movement of air from one organism to another
questions.explanation: The cycle of matter in an ecosystem refers to the movement of matter from one organism to another.

questions.question: What is the relationship between plants and energy obtained from the sun?
options: A. Plants use energy from the sun to produce oxygen
B. Plants use energy from the sun to make their own food
C. Plants use energy from the sun to break down dead organisms
D. Plants use energy from the sun to produce water
questions.explanation: Plants use energy from the sun to make their own food through photosynthesis.

questions.question: What happens to a plant if it is placed in a very hot and dry environment?
questions.explanation: Plants need water to survive, and if the environment is hot and dry, they will likely wilt and die.

questions.question: A bird builds a nest in a tree to protect its eggs from predators. What is the main reason for this behavior?
options: To keep its eggs warm and safe
To make the tree look pretty
questions.explanation: Birds build nests to protect their eggs from predators and keep them warm and safe.

questions.question: A fish lives in a coral reef and has sharp scales to protect itself from predators. What is the main reason for this adaptation?

title: 1 - Science (Grade 1)
outcomeText: 6-5-1-4-1 Identifying the inheritance of traits, explaining the variation in them, tracing their transmission from one generation to the next, distinguishing their types (dominant and recessive), and clarifying the impact of the environment on them.

questions.question: What is the main reason for variation in inherited traits?
options: B. Because of the food we eat
C. Because of a pattern of variation between inherited traits in living organisms of the same species
D. Because of the air we breathe
questions.explanation: Inherited traits can vary between living organisms of the same species due to a pattern of variation. This variation can be seen in characteristics such as eye color, hair color, and height.

questions.question: Why do living organisms of the same species look different?
options: A. Because they come from different planets
B. Because they have different parents
C. Because of a pattern of variation between inherited traits in living organisms of the same species
D. Because they are from different species
questions.explanation: Living organisms of the same species can look different due to a pattern of variation between inherited traits. This variation can result in different characteristics such as eye color, hair color, and height.

questions.question: What is the term for the way characteristics are passed down from parents to offspring?
questions.explanation: Inheritance is the process by which characteristics are passed down from parents to offspring through genes.

questions.question: How do living organisms of the same species get their traits?
options: C. From a combination of the environment and their parents
questions.explanation: Living organisms of the same species get their traits from their parents through the process of inheritance.

questions.question: What is the term for a characteristic that is always visible and will be passed down to offspring?
questions.explanation: A dominant trait is a characteristic that is always visible and will be passed down to offspring.

questions.question: What is the term for a characteristic that is not always visible and may or may not be passed down to offspring?
questions.explanation: A recessive trait is a characteristic that is not always visible and may or may not be passed down to offspring.

questions.question: How do the environment and genetics work together to affect traits?
options: A. The environment has no effect on traits
B. Genetics has no effect on traits
C. The environment and genetics work together to affect traits
D. The environment and genetics have no effect on traits
questions.explanation: The environment and genetics work together to affect traits. The environment can influence the expression of traits, while genetics determines the potential for traits to be expressed.

questions.question: What is the term for the study of how traits are passed down from parents to offspring?
questions.explanation: Genetics is the study of how traits are passed down from parents to offspring through genes.

questions.question: Why is it important to understand how traits are inherited?
options: A. So we can understand how to make new species
B. So we can understand how to change the environment
C. So we can understand how traits are passed down from parents to offspring
D. So we can understand how to make new traits
questions.explanation: Understanding how traits are inherited is important because it helps us understand how characteristics are passed down from parents to offspring and how they are affected by the environment.

title: 2 - Science (Grade 1)
outcomeText: 6-5-1-4-1 Identifying the inheritance of traits, explaining the variation in them, tracing their transmission from one generation to the next, distinguishing their types (dominant and recessive), and clarifying the impact of the environment on them.

questions.question: What is used to track the transmission of inherited traits from parents to offspring?
questions.explanation: A pedigree chart is a diagram used to show the inheritance of traits from parents to offspring.

questions.question: Which of the following traits may be dominant or recessive?
questions.explanation: Eye color is a trait that can be dominant or recessive, depending on the genotype of the individual.

questions.question: What is the term for a trait that will be expressed if an individual has one or two copies of the dominant allele?
questions.explanation: A dominant trait will be expressed if an individual has one or two copies of the dominant allele.

questions.question: What is the term for a trait that will only be expressed if an individual has two copies of the recessive allele?
questions.explanation: A recessive trait will only be expressed if an individual has two copies of the recessive allele.

questions.question: What is the term for an individual who has two different alleles for a particular trait?
questions.explanation: A heterozygous individual has two different alleles for a particular trait.

questions.question: What can affect the expression of a trait?
questions.explanation: Both genetics and environment can affect the expression of a trait.

questions.question: What is the term for a trait that is passed down from parents to offspring through genes?
questions.explanation: An inherited trait is a trait that is passed down from parents to offspring through genes.

questions.question: What is the term for a trait that is not passed down from parents to offspring through genes?
questions.explanation: An acquired trait is a trait that is not passed down from parents to offspring through genes.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a pedigree chart?
options: To track the transmission of inherited traits
To predict the likelihood of a trait being passed down
To identify the genotype of an individual
questions.explanation: A pedigree chart is used to track the transmission of inherited traits from parents to offspring.

questions.question: What is the term for a trait that can be influenced by the environment?
questions.explanation: An acquired trait is a trait that can be influenced by the environment.

title: 3 - Science (Grade 1)
outcomeText: 6-5-1-4-1 Identifying the inheritance of traits, explaining the variation in them, tracing their transmission from one generation to the next, distinguishing their types (dominant and recessive), and clarifying the impact of the environment on them.

questions.question: What is a dominant trait?
options: A trait that is not passed on to the next generation
A trait that is always expressed
A trait that is never expressed
A trait that is passed on to the next generation only sometimes
questions.explanation: A dominant trait is a trait that is always expressed in an individual, even if they have a recessive version of the same trait.

questions.question: What is a recessive trait?
options: A trait that is always expressed
A trait that is never expressed
A trait that is passed on to the next generation only sometimes
A trait that is masked by a dominant trait
questions.explanation: A recessive trait is a trait that is never expressed in an individual, unless they have two copies of the recessive version of the trait.

questions.question: What symbol is used to represent a dominant trait?
questions.explanation: The uppercase letter 'D' is often used to represent a dominant trait.

questions.question: What symbol is used to represent a recessive trait?
questions.explanation: The lowercase letter 'r' is often used to represent a recessive trait.

questions.question: What is an example of a dominant trait?
questions.explanation: Height is an example of a dominant trait, as it is always expressed in an individual, even if they have a recessive version of the trait.

questions.question: What is an example of a recessive trait?
questions.explanation: Blue eyes are an example of a recessive trait, as they are never expressed in an individual, unless they have two copies of the recessive version of the trait.

questions.question: What is the result of a dominant and recessive trait being combined?
questions.explanation: When a dominant and recessive trait are combined, the dominant trait is always expressed.

questions.question: What is the result of two recessive traits being combined?
questions.explanation: When two recessive traits are combined, neither trait is expressed.

questions.question: What is the result of a dominant and recessive trait being combined in a parent?
options: The dominant trait is passed on to the offspring
The recessive trait is passed on to the offspring
Both traits are passed on to the offspring
Neither trait is passed on to the offspring
questions.explanation: When a dominant and recessive trait are combined in a parent, the dominant trait is always passed on to the offspring.

questions.question: What is the result of two dominant traits being combined?
questions.explanation: When two dominant traits are combined, both traits are expressed.

title: 4 - Science (Grade 1)
outcomeText: 6-5-1-4-1 Identifying the inheritance of traits, explaining the variation in them, tracing their transmission from one generation to the next, distinguishing their types (dominant and recessive), and clarifying the impact of the environment on them.

questions.question: What are traits that we get from our parents called?
questions.explanation: Genetic traits are characteristics that are passed down from parents to their offspring through genes.

questions.question: What happens when a parent has a dominant trait?
options: A. The child will always get the recessive trait
B. The child will always get the dominant trait
C. The child will get either trait
D. The child will not get the trait
questions.explanation: A dominant trait will always be expressed in the offspring if the parent has the dominant trait.

questions.question: Are traits that we get from our environment considered genetic?
questions.explanation: Acquired traits are characteristics that we get from our environment, not from our parents.

questions.question: What are traits that we get from our parents and are always expressed?
questions.explanation: Dominant traits are characteristics that are always expressed in the offspring if the parent has the dominant trait.

questions.question: Can a child have a trait that they don't get from either parent?
questions.explanation: A child can have a trait that they don't get from either parent due to environmental factors.

questions.question: What are traits that we get from our parents and can be hidden?
questions.explanation: Recessive traits are characteristics that can be hidden in the offspring if the parent has the recessive trait.

questions.question: Can a child get a trait from only one parent?
questions.explanation: A child can get a trait from only one parent if the other parent has the recessive trait.

questions.question: What are traits that we get from our environment and are not passed down from parents?
questions.explanation: Acquired traits are characteristics that we get from our environment, not from our parents.

questions.question: Can a child have a dominant trait if neither parent has the dominant trait?
questions.explanation: A child can have a dominant trait if they inherit two dominant traits, one from each parent.

questions.question: What are traits that we get from our parents and can be influenced by the environment?
questions.explanation: Genetic traits can be influenced by environmental factors, such as nutrition and health.

questions.explanation: When you heat a liquid, it turns into a gas.

questions.explanation: The composition of a substance remains the same during a chemical reaction.

questions.explanation: When you cool a gas, it turns into a liquid.

questions.question: What happens when you mix a solid and a liquid?
questions.explanation: When you mix a solid and a liquid, they form a mixture.

questions.question: What is the result of heating a mixture?
questions.explanation: When you heat a mixture, the different components may change state.

questions.explanation: The properties of a substance may change during a chemical reaction, but the mass remains preserved.

title: 4 - Science (Grade 1)
outcomeText: 6-5-2-1-1 Exploring the physical properties of matter, distinguishing the molecular structure of its different states, and explaining the change of matter states due to heat.

questions.question: What is the state of matter that keeps its shape and volume when it is poured?
questions.explanation: Matter that keeps its shape and volume when it is poured is liquid.

questions.question: What is the state of matter that has a fixed shape and volume?
questions.explanation: Matter that has a fixed shape and volume is solid.

questions.question: What is the state of matter that has neither a fixed shape nor a fixed volume?
questions.explanation: Matter that has neither a fixed shape nor a fixed volume is gas.

questions.question: What happens to the shape of water when it is heated?
questions.explanation: When water is heated, its molecules move faster, causing it to expand and change shape.

questions.question: What is the force that keeps molecules together in a solid?
questions.explanation: The force that keeps molecules together in a solid is attraction.

questions.question: What is the state of matter that is made up of tiny particles that are free to move?

questions.question: What is the effect of decreasing temperature on the movement of molecules in a substance?

questions.question: What is the state of matter that has a fixed shape but not a fixed volume?
questions.explanation: Matter that has a fixed shape but not a fixed volume is plasma.

questions.question: What is the force that holds molecules together in a liquid?
questions.explanation: The force that holds molecules together in a liquid is gravity.

title: 1 - Science (Grade 1)
outcomeText: 6-5-2-1-2 Distinguishing between compounds and mixtures and their types, explaining chemical changes of matter and the related concepts and methods, and comparing the masses of substances when their properties change based on the law of conservation of mass.

questions.question: What is a chemical bond?
options: A force that holds atoms together in a substance
A process that changes the properties of a substance
questions.explanation: A chemical bond is a force that holds atoms together in a substance.

questions.question: What happens to the properties of a substance when a chemical bond is formed?
questions.explanation: The properties of a substance change when a chemical bond is formed.

questions.question: What is an indicator of a chemical reaction?
questions.explanation: A change in color, a change in temperature, and a change in smell are all indicators of a chemical reaction.

questions.question: What is a chemical reaction?
options: A change in the properties of a substance
A change in the shape of a substance
A change in the size of a substance
A change in the color of a substance
questions.explanation: A chemical reaction is a change in the properties of a substance.

questions.question: What is the role of a catalyst in a chemical reaction?
options: To change the properties of the substance
questions.explanation: A catalyst is a substance that speeds up a chemical reaction.

questions.question: What is a mixture?
options: A substance that is made up of only one type of atom
A substance that is made up of two or more types of atoms
A substance that is made up of only one type of molecule
A substance that is made up of two or more types of molecules
questions.explanation: A mixture is a substance that is made up of two or more types of atoms.

questions.question: What is a compound?
options: A substance that is made up of only one type of atom
A substance that is made up of two or more types of atoms
A substance that is made up of only one type of molecule
A substance that is made up of two or more types of molecules
questions.explanation: A compound is a substance that is made up of two or more types of atoms.

questions.question: What is the law of conservation of mass?
options: The mass of a substance can change during a chemical reaction
The mass of a substance cannot change during a chemical reaction
The mass of a substance can increase during a chemical reaction
The mass of a substance can decrease during a chemical reaction
questions.explanation: The law of conservation of mass states that the mass of a substance cannot change during a chemical reaction.

questions.question: What happens to the mass of a substance during a chemical reaction?
questions.explanation: The mass of a substance stays the same during a chemical reaction.

questions.question: What is the result of a chemical reaction?
options: A change in the properties of a substance
A change in the shape of a substance
A change in the size of a substance
A change in the color of a substance
questions.explanation: The result of a chemical reaction is a change in the properties of a substance.

title: 2 - Science (Grade 1)

questions.question: What is a mixture?
options: A substance made up of only one type of particle
A substance made up of two or more different substances
A substance that can be broken down into simpler substances
A substance that cannot be broken down into simpler substances
questions.explanation: A mixture is a substance made up of two or more different substances.

questions.question: What is a compound?
options: A substance made up of two or more different substances
A substance made up of only one type of particle
A substance that can be broken down into simpler substances
A substance that cannot be broken down into simpler substances
questions.explanation: A compound is a substance made up of only one type of particle.

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of a mixture?
questions.explanation: Air is an example of a mixture because it is made up of different gases.

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of a compound?
questions.explanation: Water is an example of a compound because it is made up of two hydrogen atoms and one oxygen atom.

questions.question: What is a solution?
options: A mixture of two or more substances that are not mixed together
A mixture of two or more substances that are mixed together
A compound made up of two or more substances
A substance made up of only one type of particle
questions.explanation: A solution is a mixture of two or more substances that are mixed together.

questions.question: What is a suspension?
options: A mixture of two or more substances that are not mixed together
A mixture of two or more substances that are mixed together
A compound made up of two or more substances
A substance made up of two or more particles that are not mixed together
questions.explanation: A suspension is a mixture of two or more substances that are not mixed together.

questions.question: What is a colloid?
options: A mixture of two or more substances that are not mixed together
A mixture of two or more substances that are mixed together
A compound made up of two or more substances
A substance made up of two or more particles that are dispersed in another substance
questions.explanation: A colloid is a substance made up of two or more particles that are dispersed in another substance.

questions.question: What is a homogeneous mixture?
options: A mixture of two or more substances that are not mixed together
A mixture of two or more substances that are mixed together
A compound made up of two or more substances
A mixture of two or more substances that are uniform throughout
questions.explanation: A homogeneous mixture is a mixture of two or more substances that are uniform throughout.

questions.question: What is a heterogeneous mixture?
options: A mixture of two or more substances that are not mixed together
A mixture of two or more substances that are mixed together
A compound made up of two or more substances
A mixture of two or more substances that are not uniform throughout
questions.explanation: A heterogeneous mixture is a mixture of two or more substances that are not uniform throughout.

questions.question: What is a mixture of oil and water?
questions.explanation: A mixture of oil and water is a colloid because the oil droplets are dispersed in the water.

title: 3 - Science (Grade 1)
outcomeText: 6-5-2-1-2 Distinguishing between compounds and mixtures and their types, explaining chemical changes of matter and the related concepts and methods, and comparing the masses of substances when their properties change based on the law of conservation of mass.

questions.question: What is a solution?
options: A mixture of two or more substances.
A substance that cannot be broken down into simpler substances.
A liquid that dissolves in another liquid.
questions.explanation: A solution is a mixture of two or more substances.

questions.question: What are the parts of a solution?
questions.explanation: A solution has two main parts: solvent and solute.

questions.question: What is the concentration of a solution?
options: The amount of solute in a solution.
The amount of solvent in a solution.
The amount of solution in a container.
The amount of a substance in a solution.

questions.question: What is a concentrated solution?

A solution with a large amount of solute.
A solution with a small amount of solvent.
A solution with a large amount of solvent.

questions.question: What is a dilute solution?

A solution with a small amount of solute.
A solution with a small amount of solvent.
A solution with a large amount of solvent.

questions.question: What is a saturated solution?
options: A solution with a large amount of solute.
A solution with a small amount of solute.
A solution with a large amount of solvent.
A solution with a small amount of solvent.

questions.question: What is an unsaturated solution?

A solution with a small amount of solute.
A solution with a large amount of solvent.
A solution with a small amount of solvent.

title: 1 - Science (Grade 1)

questions.question: What is a chemical element?
options: A mixture of two or more elements
A pure substance that consists of only one type of atom
A compound made up of two or more elements
A physical property of an element

options: A chemical element is a compound made up of two or more elements
A compound is a mixture of two or more elements
A chemical element is a pure substance that consists of only one type of atom
A compound is a physical property of an element

questions.question: What happens to the temperature of the surroundings when a chemical reaction is endothermic?
options: The temperature of the surroundings increases
The temperature of the surroundings decreases
The temperature of the surroundings remains the same
The temperature of the surroundings is not affected

questions.question: Which type of reaction releases heat into the surroundings?

The temperature of the surroundings remains the same
The temperature of the surroundings increases
The temperature of the surroundings is not affected

questions.question: What happens when two forces are equal and opposite?
questions.explanation: When two forces are equal and opposite, they cancel each other out and do not affect the motion of the object.

questions.question: What is an example of a balanced force?
options: A. A person pushing a wall.
B. A person pulling a rope.
C. Two people pulling a sled in the same direction.
D. A person pushing a car up a hill.
questions.explanation: When two forces are equal and opposite, they are balanced and do not affect the motion of the object.

questions.question: What is an example of an unbalanced force?
options: A. A person pushing a ball.
B. A person pulling a rope.
C. Two people pulling a sled in the same direction.
questions.explanation: When one force is greater than another, it is unbalanced and affects the motion of the object.

questions.question: What happens when an unbalanced force acts on an object?
questions.explanation: When an unbalanced force acts on an object, it causes the object to move.

questions.question: What is an example of an unbalanced force in everyday life?
options: A. A person walking down the street.
C. A car driving on the road.
D. A person pushing a wall.
questions.explanation: When a car drives on the road, it is an example of an unbalanced force because the force of the engine is greater than the force of friction.

questions.question: What happens when a balanced force acts on an object?
questions.explanation: When a balanced force acts on an object, it does not affect the motion of the object.

questions.question: What is the effect of an unbalanced force on the motion of an object?
questions.explanation: When an unbalanced force acts on an object, it causes the object to move.

questions.question: What is the effect of a balanced force on the motion of an object?
questions.explanation: When a balanced force acts on an object, it does not affect the motion of the object.

questions.question: What is an example of a balanced force in everyday life?
options: A. A person pushing a wall.
B. A person pulling a rope.
C. Two people pulling a sled in the same direction.
questions.explanation: When two people pull a sled in the same direction, it is an example of a balanced force because the forces are equal and opposite.

questions.question: What is the difference between a balanced and an unbalanced force?
options: A. A balanced force is greater than an unbalanced force.
B. An unbalanced force is greater than a balanced force.
C. A balanced force is equal to an unbalanced force.
D. A balanced force is opposite to an unbalanced force.
questions.explanation: A balanced force is equal to an unbalanced force, but in opposite directions.

title: 1 - Science (Grade 1)
outcomeText: 6-5-2-3-2 Understanding the principle of energy conservation during its transformations and apply it in daily life.

questions.question: What is a wave?
options: A disturbance that travels through a medium
questions.explanation: A wave is a disturbance that travels through a medium, like water or air.

questions.question: How can you represent a wave graphically?
questions.explanation: A wave can be represented graphically by drawing a wavy line, which shows the disturbance traveling through the medium.

questions.question: What is an example of a wave in our daily life?
options: A book falling off a table
A river flowing through a valley
A wave crashing on the beach
A tree swaying in the wind
questions.explanation: A wave crashing on the beach is an example of a wave in our daily life, where the disturbance of the water travels through the air and onto the shore.

questions.question: What is the principle of energy conservation?
options: Energy can be created or destroyed
Energy can be transferred from one place to another
Energy can be changed from one form to another
questions.explanation: The principle of energy conservation states that energy can be changed from one form to another, but it cannot be created or destroyed.

questions.question: What is an example of energy being transformed?
options: A car moving from one place to another
A piece of wood burning in a fire
A rock sitting on the ground
questions.explanation: A piece of wood burning in a fire is an example of energy being transformed, where the chemical energy in the wood is changed into heat and light energy.

questions.question: Can energy be created or destroyed?
questions.explanation: No, according to the principle of energy conservation, energy cannot be created or destroyed, only transformed from one form to another.

questions.question: What is an example of energy being transferred?
options: A ball being thrown from one person to another
A book falling off a table
A car moving from one place to another
A piece of wood burning in a fire
questions.explanation: A ball being thrown from one person to another is an example of energy being transferred, where the kinetic energy of the ball is transferred from one person to another.

questions.question: What is the difference between a wave and a vibration?
options: A wave travels through a medium, while a vibration does not
A wave travels through a medium, while a vibration travels through a solid
A wave is a disturbance that travels through a medium, while a vibration is a back-and-forth motion
A wave is a back-and-forth motion, while a vibration is a disturbance that travels through a medium
questions.explanation: A wave is a disturbance that travels through a medium, while a vibration is a back-and-forth motion, like the vibration of a guitar string.

questions.question: Can a wave be transferred from one place to another?
questions.explanation: Yes, a wave can be transferred from one place to another, like a wave in the ocean being transferred to the shore.

questions.question: What is an example of a wave in nature?
options: A river flowing through a valley
A wave crashing on the beach
questions.explanation: A wave crashing on the beach is an example of a wave in nature, where the disturbance of the water travels through the air and onto the shore.

outcomeText: 6-5-2-4-1 Describing waves, distinguishing between their properties theoretically and graphically, and predicting their movement.

questions.question: What type of wave can we hear?
questions.explanation: Sound waves are a type of wave that we can hear.

questions.question: What is the main difference between a sound wave and a light wave?
questions.explanation: Light waves are invisible, whereas sound waves are not.

questions.question: Which type of wave can travel through a vacuum?
questions.explanation: Light waves can travel through a vacuum, whereas sound waves cannot.

questions.question: What is the property of a sound wave that allows us to hear it?
questions.explanation: Frequency is the property of a sound wave that allows us to hear it.

questions.question: Which type of wave is used for communication?
questions.explanation: Sound waves are often used for communication, such as through speech.

questions.question: What is the property of a light wave that gives it its color?
questions.explanation: Frequency is the property of a light wave that gives it its color.

questions.question: Can sound waves travel through a vacuum?
questions.explanation: Sound waves require a medium to travel, so they cannot travel through a vacuum.

questions.question: Which type of wave is used for transportation?
questions.explanation: Water waves are often used for transportation, such as through boats.

questions.question: What is the property of a light wave that gives it its brightness?
questions.explanation: Amplitude is the property of a light wave that gives it its brightness.

questions.question: Can light waves travel through water?
questions.explanation: Light waves can travel through water, but they are refracted, or bent.

title: 3 - Science (Grade 1)
outcomeText: 6-5-2-4-1 Describing waves, distinguishing between their properties theoretically and graphically, and predicting their movement.

questions.question: What happens to a wave when it hits a solid object?
questions.explanation: The wave bounces back.

questions.question: What happens to a wave when it passes through a narrow opening?
questions.explanation: The wave spreads out and gets wider.

questions.question: What happens to a wave when it hits a soft surface?
questions.explanation: The wave slows down and gets weaker.

questions.question: What happens to a wave when it is exposed to strong winds?
questions.explanation: The wave gets bigger and stronger.

questions.question: What happens to a wave when it hits a sandy beach?
questions.explanation: The wave loses some of its energy and breaks.

questions.question: What happens to a wave when it passes through a narrow channel?
questions.explanation: The wave speeds up and gets narrower.

questions.question: What happens to a wave when it is exposed to a calm sea?
questions.explanation: The wave gets smaller and weaker.

questions.question: What happens to a wave when it hits a rocky shore?
questions.explanation: The wave breaks and crashes against the shore.

questions.question: What happens to a wave when it is exposed to a strong current?
questions.explanation: The wave changes direction and moves in a different way.

questions.question: What happens to a wave when it passes through a wide, open space?
questions.explanation: The wave spreads out and gets wider.

title: 2 - Science (Grade 1)
outcomeText: 6-5-2-4-2 Understanding the concepts of reflection and refraction of light, transmission of sound, and explaining their role in interaction and communication in the surrounding environment.

questions.question: What is the main function of the eye?
questions.explanation: The eye helps us to see the world around us.

questions.question: Why can't we see in complete darkness?
questions.explanation: We need light to see, and in complete darkness, there is no light to reflect off objects.

questions.question: What happens when light passes from air into a prism?
questions.explanation: When light passes from air into a prism, it breaks into different colors, a process known as refraction.

questions.question: How do we see colors around us?
questions.explanation: We see colors around us because of our eyes, which detect the different wavelengths of light.

questions.question: What is the process called when light bends as it passes from one medium to another?
questions.explanation: The process of light bending as it passes from one medium to another is called refraction.

questions.question: Why can we see objects clearly when they are far away?
options: Because of the light from the sun
Because of the way our eyes focus
questions.explanation: We can see objects clearly when they are far away because of the way our eyes focus, which allows us to see them in sharp detail.

questions.question: What happens to light when it hits a smooth surface?
questions.explanation: When light hits a smooth surface, it bounces back, a process known as reflection.

questions.question: Why can't we see objects that are very far away?
options: Because of the way our eyes focus
Because of the light from the stars
questions.explanation: We can't see objects that are very far away because of the way our eyes focus, which has a limited range.

questions.question: What is the role of the eye's lens in seeing objects?
options: To move the object farther away
To focus the light onto the retina
To block the light from entering the eye
questions.explanation: The eye's lens plays a crucial role in seeing objects by focusing the light onto the retina, allowing us to see them clearly.

questions.question: Why do we see different colors when we look at a rainbow?
options: Because of the different temperatures of the air
Because of the different wavelengths of light
Because of the different shapes of the raindrops
Because of the different sizes of the raindrops
questions.explanation: We see different colors when we look at a rainbow because of the different wavelengths of light, which are separated and refracted as they pass through the raindrops.

title: 2 - Science (Grade 1)
outcomeText: 6-5-2-5-1Understanding the concept of electric charge, explaining the attraction and repulsion of charged bodies, and comparing electrical circuits connected in series and in parallel.

questions.question: What happens when a charged object is brought near another charged object?
questions.explanation: Charged objects repel each other if they have the same type of charge.

questions.question: What type of circuit allows electricity to flow through multiple paths?
questions.explanation: A parallel circuit allows electricity to flow through multiple paths, making it more efficient.

questions.question: What is the flow of electrons in a circuit called?
questions.explanation: Electric current is the flow of electrons in a circuit.

questions.question: What happens when a circuit is closed?
options: A. Electricity flows through the circuit
B. Electricity does not flow through the circuit
questions.explanation: When a circuit is closed, electricity flows through the circuit.

questions.question: What is the difference between a series circuit and a parallel circuit?
options: A. A series circuit has more paths for electricity to flow
B. A parallel circuit has more paths for electricity to flow
C. A series circuit has one path for electricity to flow
D. A parallel circuit has one path for electricity to flow
questions.explanation: A series circuit has one path for electricity to flow, while a parallel circuit has multiple paths.

questions.question: What happens when a charged object is rubbed against another object?
options: C. The objects do not change
questions.explanation: When a charged object is rubbed against another object, the second object becomes charged.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a fuse in an electrical circuit?
options: A. To increase the voltage in the circuit
B. To decrease the current in the circuit
C. To prevent overloading in the circuit
D. To increase the resistance in the circuit
questions.explanation: A fuse is used to prevent overloading in an electrical circuit.

questions.question: What type of circuit has only one path for electricity to flow?
questions.explanation: A series circuit has only one path for electricity to flow.

questions.question: What happens when a circuit is open?
options: A. Electricity flows through the circuit
B. Electricity does not flow through the circuit
questions.explanation: When a circuit is open, electricity does not flow through the circuit.

questions.question: What is the unit of measurement for electric current?
questions.explanation: Ampere (A) is the unit of measurement for electric current.

title: 3 - Science (Grade 1)
outcomeText: 6-5-2-5-1Understanding the concept of electric charge, explaining the attraction and repulsion of charged bodies, and comparing electrical circuits connected in series and in parallel.

questions.question: What happens when two objects with the same electric charge touch each other?
questions.explanation: When two objects with the same electric charge touch each other, they repel each other.

questions.question: What kind of circuit is made when all the devices are connected one after the other?
questions.explanation: A series circuit is made when all the devices are connected one after the other.

questions.question: Why do objects with opposite electric charges attract each other?
questions.explanation: Objects with opposite electric charges attract each other because they neutralize each other's charge.

questions.question: What happens when a device is connected in a series circuit?
questions.explanation: When a device is connected in a series circuit, the flow of electricity is blocked if any device in the circuit is faulty.

questions.question: What is the main difference between a series and parallel circuit?
questions.explanation: The main difference between a series and parallel circuit is the flow of electricity. In a series circuit, the flow of electricity is continuous, while in a parallel circuit, the flow of electricity is divided among the devices.

questions.question: What happens when a device is connected in a parallel circuit?
questions.explanation: When a device is connected in a parallel circuit, it can still function even if one of the other devices in the circuit is faulty.

questions.question: Why do objects with the same electric charge repel each other?
questions.explanation: Objects with the same electric charge repel each other because they both have an excess of electrons or an excess of protons.

questions.question: What is the result of connecting a light bulb and a battery in a series circuit?
questions.explanation: The result of connecting a light bulb and a battery in a series circuit is that the light bulb will light up.

questions.question: What is the advantage of using a parallel circuit?
questions.explanation: The advantage of using a parallel circuit is that it allows multiple devices to be connected and still function even if one of the devices is faulty.

questions.question: What happens when a device is connected in a circuit with a faulty device?
questions.explanation: When a device is connected in a circuit with a faulty device, the flow of electricity is blocked and the device will not function.

title: 1 - English (Grade 1)
outcomeText: 9.2.1.1.2 Eliciting the main and sub-ideas of a long and complex text, deducing implicit ideas, distinguishing, interpreting, and analyzing them.

questions.question: What is the main idea of a story about a boy who goes to the park?
options: He played soccer with his friends.
He rode a bike in the park.
He went to the park to play.
questions.explanation: The main idea is the overall purpose of the story, which is the boy's visit to the park.

questions.question: What is the chronological order of events in a story about a boy who wakes up, eats breakfast, and goes to school?
options: Eats breakfast, goes to school, wakes up.
Wakes up, eats breakfast, goes to school.
Eats breakfast, sleeps, goes to school.
questions.explanation: Chronological order refers to the sequence of events in the correct order of time.

questions.question: What is the spatial order of events in a story about a boy who goes to the beach, plays in the sand, and swims in the ocean?
options: He goes to the beach, plays in the sand, swims in the ocean.
He swims in the ocean, plays in the sand, goes to the beach.
He plays in the sand, goes to the beach, swims in the ocean.
He swims in the ocean, goes to the beach, plays in the sand.
questions.explanation: Spatial order refers to the sequence of events in the correct order of location.

questions.question: What is the main idea of a story about a boy who has a pet dog?
options: The boy has a pet cat.
The boy has a pet dog.
The boy likes to play outside.
The boy has a pet bird.
questions.explanation: The main idea is the overall purpose of the story, which is the boy's pet dog.

questions.question: What is the supporting evidence in a story about a boy who built a sandcastle?
options: He used a bucket to carry water.
He used a shovel to dig sand.
He built a sandcastle in the park.
He used a stick to make a flag.
questions.explanation: Supporting evidence refers to the details that prove the main idea or claim.

questions.question: What is the main idea of a story about a boy who went to the library?
options: The boy went to the park.
The boy went to the library.
The boy likes to read books.
The boy went to the store.
questions.explanation: The main idea is the overall purpose of the story, which is the boy's visit to the library.

questions.question: What is the chronological order of events in a story about a boy who eats breakfast, goes to school, and plays soccer?
options: Eats breakfast, goes to school, plays soccer.
Plays soccer, eats breakfast, goes to school.
Eats breakfast, goes to school, sleeps.
questions.explanation: Chronological order refers to the sequence of events in the correct order of time.

questions.question: What is the spatial order of events in a story about a boy who goes to the store, buys a toy, and goes home?
options: He goes to the store, buys a toy, goes home.
He buys a toy, goes to the store, goes home.
He goes home, buys a toy, goes to the store.
He goes to the store, goes home, buys a toy.
questions.explanation: Spatial order refers to the sequence of events in the correct order of location.

questions.question: What is the main idea of a story about a boy who has a pet fish?
options: The boy has a pet cat.
The boy has a pet dog.
The boy has a pet fish.
The boy likes to play outside.
questions.explanation: The main idea is the overall purpose of the story, which is the boy's pet fish.

questions.question: What is the supporting evidence in a story about a girl who built a birdhouse?
options: She used a hammer to hit nails.
She used a saw to cut wood.
She built a birdhouse in the park.
She used a screwdriver to tighten screws.
questions.explanation: Supporting evidence refers to the details that prove the main idea or claim.

questions.question: What is the main idea of a text about a sunny day?
questions.explanation: The main idea of the text is the weather, which is sunny.

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of a point of similarity between two stories about animals?
questions.explanation: Both stories are about animals that have four legs.

questions.question: What is the difference between a story about a cat and a story about a dog?
options: They are both the same animal
questions.explanation: Cats and dogs are different animals with different characteristics.

questions.question: How can you compare two texts about the same topic?
options: By identifying the points of similarity and difference
questions.explanation: Comparing two texts about the same topic involves identifying the points of similarity and difference.

questions.question: What is the strength of the evidence in a text about the importance of recycling?
questions.explanation: The text provides strong evidence about the importance of recycling.

questions.question: How can you distinguish between two texts with different styles?
options: By analyzing the language and tone
questions.explanation: Two texts with different styles can be distinguished by analyzing the language and tone.

questions.question: What is an example of a point of difference between two texts about the same topic?
options: They are both the same animal
questions.explanation: Two texts about the same topic can have different characteristics.

questions.question: How can you analyze a text about a topic you are interested in?
options: By identifying the points of similarity and difference
questions.explanation: Analyzing a text involves reading it carefully and identifying the main ideas.

questions.question: What is the type of example in a text about the importance of saving water?
questions.explanation: The text provides a strong example about the importance of saving water.

questions.question: How can you interpret a text about a topic you are not familiar with?
questions.explanation: Interpreting a text involves reading it carefully and asking questions if needed.

questions.question: What is the main idea of a story about a boy who goes to the park and sees a big tree?
options: The boy likes to play in the park.
The boy sees a big tree in the park.
The boy is happy when he is in the park.
The boy is sad because it is raining.
questions.explanation: The main idea of a story is the central or most important idea that the story is about.

questions.question: What is the relationship between the sun and the day?
questions.explanation: The sun is a star that gives us light and makes the day.

questions.question: How do the characters in a story feel when they are happy?
questions.explanation: When characters in a story feel happy, they are described as being happy.

questions.question: What is the connection between a book and a library?
questions.explanation: A library is a place where books are kept, and people can borrow them.

questions.question: What is the main idea of a story about a girl who has a pet dog?
options: The girl likes to play with her dog.
The girl has a pet dog.
The girl is happy when she is with her dog.
The girl is sad because her dog is sick.
questions.explanation: The main idea of a story is the central or most important idea that the story is about.

questions.question: How do the characters in a story feel when they are scared?
questions.explanation: When characters in a story feel scared, they are described as being scared.

questions.question: What is the connection between a picture and a story?

questions.question: What is the main idea of a story about a boy who learns to ride a bike?

questions.question: What do we learn about the importance of sharing in the story?
questions.explanation: The story teaches us that sharing is important because it helps others feel happy and included.

questions.question: Why is it good to help others in need?
questions.explanation: It makes others feel happy and shows that we care about them.

questions.question: What do we learn about the value of kindness in the story?
questions.explanation: The story teaches us that kindness makes others feel happy and special.

questions.question: Why is it important to respect others' feelings?

questions.question: What do we learn about the value of honesty in the story?

questions.question: Why is it good to be kind to animals?

questions.question: What do we learn about the value of hard work in the story?

questions.question: Why is it important to be polite to others?

questions.question: What do we learn about the value of friendship in the story?
questions.explanation: The story teaches us that friendship is important because it helps us feel happy and included.

questions.question: Why is it good to help the environment?
questions.explanation: It helps us keep our planet clean and shows that we care about the future.

questions.question: What does the author want to tell us in the story?
options: He wants us to be happy
He wants us to learn about the world
He wants us to be kind to animals
He wants us to be sad

questions.question: Why do you think the author wrote this story?

questions.question: What do you think the author is trying to say about sharing?
options: That it is bad to share
That it is good to share
That it doesn't matter if we share or not
That we should only share with our friends

questions.question: Why do you think the author used the word 'happy' in the story?
questions.explanation: The author used the word 'happy' to make us feel happy and to show that the story has a positive ending.

That we should only try new things at home

options: To make the story more exciting
To help us understand the story better

questions.question: What do you think the author is trying to say about being responsible?

That it is only important at school

questions.question: What can we do if we find a problem in our community?
options: B. We can talk to our friends about it.
C. We can think of a solution and tell others.
D. We can run away from it.
questions.explanation: We can think of a solution and tell others.

questions.question: Why is it important to express our opinion?
options: A. So that others can agree with us.
B. So that others can disagree with us.
C. So that we can share our thoughts and ideas.
D. So that we can be quiet and listen.
questions.explanation: So that we can share our thoughts and ideas.

questions.question: What can we do if we find a problem in our school?
options: A. We can tell the teacher but not do anything.
B. We can think of a solution and tell others.
C. We can ignore it and hope it goes away.
D. We can run away from it.
questions.explanation: We can think of a solution and tell others.

options: A. So that we can always be right.
B. So that we can disagree with them.
C. So that we can learn from their experiences.
D. So that we can ignore them.

questions.question: What can we do if we find a problem in our family?

C. We can run away from it.

questions.question: Why is it important to think critically?
options: A. So that we can always be right.
B. So that we can follow what others say.
C. So that we can make informed decisions.
D. So that we can be lazy.

D. So that we can be quiet and listen.

options: A. We can ignore it and hope it goes away.
B. We can think of a solution and talk to others.
C. We can run away from it.

questions.question: Why is it important to use our own experiences to solve problems?
options: A. So that we can always be right.
B. So that we can follow what others say.

D. So that we can be lazy.
